cards.front: Explain the core goal of BFS in graph problems.
cards.back: Breadth-First Search (BFS) explores a graph level by level starting from a source node. Its main goal is to find the shortest path in an unweighted graph and to systematically explore all nodes reachable from the starting node.

cards.front: Explain the core goal of DFS in graph problems.
cards.back: Depth-First Search (DFS) explores a graph by going as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. It is commonly used for path finding, checking connectivity, exploring components, and performing exhaustive searches.

Union-Find (also called Disjoint Set Union, DSU) is a data structure used to efficiently group elements into sets and check whether two elements belong to the same set (connected component). It is commonly used in graph problems such as cycle detection and Minimum Spanning Tree algorithms.

cards.front: Explain the workings of union and find in union find.
cards.back: The Union-Find structure has two main operations. Find(x) returns the representative (root) of the set that element x belongs to, usually implemented with path compression to make future lookups faster. Union(a, b) merges the sets containing elements a and b by connecting their roots, often using union by rank or size to keep the tree shallow.

cards.front: Explain what topological sort is doing.
cards.back: Topological sort produces a linear ordering of vertices in a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) such that for every directed edge u → v, node u appears before node v in the ordering. It is used to represent dependency ordering, such as task scheduling.

cards.front: Explain how topological sort works in practice (pseudo logic).
cards.back: One common approach is Kahn's Algorithm: 1) Compute the in-degree of every node. 2) Add all nodes with in-degree 0 to a queue. 3) Repeatedly remove a node from the queue, add it to the ordering, and reduce the in-degree of its neighbors. 4) If any neighbor's in-degree becomes 0, add it to the queue. Continue until all nodes are processed. If not all nodes are processed, the graph contains a cycle.

cards.front: Explain what a minimum spanning tree is.
cards.back: A Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) is a subset of edges in a weighted, connected, undirected graph that connects all vertices without cycles and with the minimum possible total edge weight.
